retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth cusps function to guide erupting teeth into appropriate occlusion with periodontal stimulation informing neural reflexes for jaw closure and mastication [1] . The maxillary palatal cusps and the mandibular facial cusps comprise the centric cusps involved in centric occlusion (discussed below). In contrast, the maxillary buccal cusp and the mandibular lingual cusps guide the mandible during the lateral excursion.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the transverse dimension, normal occlusion is when the palatine cusps of the upper molars and premolars occlude in the fossa of the lower molars and premolars. In the anteroposterior plane, the upper incisors occlude on the labial aspects of the lower incisors.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Occlusal Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Before placing the rubber dam and preparing the tooth, observe the following: Whether the opposing tooth has extruded as a consequence of long-standing carious lesions Whether there is mesial drift as a result of carious lesions leading to proximal space loss Presence of spacing and crowding Examine occlusion directly in the mouth or indirectly with dental analysis casts for incisor, canine, and molar relationship on both sides Examine for dental midline and the cusp fossa relationship bilaterally

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term buccal crossbite refers to the buccal cusps of the lower teeth, which are occluding buccal to the buccal cusps of the upper teeth. Scissor bite refers to the condition when the buccal cusps of the lower teeth occlude lingual to the lingual cusps of the upper teeth. Crossbite malocclusion can have a skeletal or dental component or a combination.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Dens evaginatus, or a “talon cusp,” presents as an outcropped growth of normal tooth structure, commonly affecting the lingual aspect of an anterior maxillary tooth. Talon cusps have been classified into three categories with distinct clinical presentations. [17] Type 1 talon cusps are a morphologically well-differentiated additional cusp that extends half the distance from the cementoenamel junction to the incisal edge. Type 2 talon cusps, known as a “semi-talon,” measure at least one millimeter and extend half the distance from the cementoenamel junction to the incisal edge. Type 2 talon cusps often blend with the surface of the crown. Type 3 talon cusps, or “trace talons,” are enlarged or prominent cingula that can be conical, bifid, or tubercle.

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Indirect cuspal coverage in the form of a crown or onlay may also function extremely well as an external splint, prohibiting the flexure of the crack. Guthrie et al observed that most cracks extended into the cervical third of the crown. In their study, they concluded that 25 of the 28 tested cracked teeth became asymptomatic after receiving a full-coverage crown. [30] A full-coverage crown allows for a more equal distribution of occlusal forces across the affected tooth due to the resistant form of the crown. This prevents the crack's flexure and inhibits it from propagating further and causing more damage. [30] However, tooth preparation for full-coverage crowns is invasive and increases the risk of devitalization. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: When in occlusion, a lateral view shows a convexity in the maxillary occlusal surface interdigitating with a concavity in the mandibular occlusal surface. The occlusal curve is called the Curve of Spee. Extending the curve while maintaining its arc into a circle centered at the glabella creates the Curve of Monson. In a coronal plane, the mandibular posterior dentition is characterized by a lingual incline, and a buccal incline characterizes the maxillary posterior dentition. The coronal occlusal curve in the posterior dentition is called the Curve of Wilson. [10] Maintaining these characteristic occlusal curves is imperative to the practice of dental implants and denture design/fitting.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- With Adjacent Stainless Steel Crowns
retrieved_docs.content: When more than 1 crown is required in a quadrant, both teeth should be prepared during the same visit. There should be slightly more than usual reduction on the adjacent proximal surfaces. This makes it easier to place multiple crowns. Before reducing another tooth, the occlusal reduction of one should be completed. Simultaneous reduction of both teeth results in improper reduction. There is a risk of mesiodistal space loss if there are proximal caries on adjacent teeth. To restore carious adjacent teeth with stainless steel crowns, the preparations should be modified to fit the teeth with smaller crowns than normal. Rather than more proximal reduction, further reduction of the buccal and lingual walls is carried out. [6] [10] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Early detection plays a crucial role in enhancing the prognosis of a cracked tooth. What initially appears as a minor, innocuous crack with mild symptoms can quickly develop into a complicated and extensive crack involving the pulp or root, rendering the tooth unrestorable. The extent, position, and direction of the crack determine the prognosis. Cracked teeth with normal pulp can be effectively managed with therapeutic measures such as direct composite splinting, placement of orthodontic bands, or indirect definitive cuspal coverage.

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth that have undergone endodontic access display more unrestorable fracture patterns compared to those that have not been accessed. [9] Beyond restorative etiological considerations, developmental and morphological factors may also contribute to a tooth's fracture risk. Occlusal anatomy characterized by deep fissures and steep cusp angles, potentially leading to altered intercuspation, pulp chamber volume changes, rotations, and inclinations, can further influence fracture risk. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Managing cracked tooth syndrome lacks a clear, one-size-fits-all approach, as treatment depends on the extent and position of the lesion. Alleviating patient symptoms when they report pain is the initial priority before considering the long-term management of the tooth. After localizing the crack, stabilization is necessary to prevent flexing under occlusal forces, alleviating patient pain. [2] In cases without symptoms of pulpal involvement, removing the existing restoration and undermined tooth tissue/cusp to "chase out" the crack may suffice. Subsequently, placing a suitable restoration, such as a flowable composite material, is recommended. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Proximal Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Proximal surfaces are reduced using a 69 L/ tapered fissure bur at high speed to make room for the crown and establish a feather finish line gingivally while avoiding damaging neighboring teeth. The mesial and distal contact points should be cleared so a probe could pass through them. Following the normal proximal contour, proximal slices should slightly converge toward the occlusal and lingual. A smooth taper from occlusal to gingival, free of ledges or shoulders, should be achieved. [10] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Microscopy, Electron
retrieved_docs.content: The first type is a shallow prism, with a round and uniform shape with sharp outlines, located at the same level of the tooth surface. The second type is a well-defined prism with an open concave center and well-defined peripheral outlines at the same surface level as the type 1 prisms. The third type of prisms are ill-defined, irregular shapes and irregular borders, defined as microporosity. The surface enamel of deciduous teeth has a so-called prismless structure with parallel crystallites and no prism boundaries, based on SEM studies. [5] The crystal arrangement creates the discontinuities, known as prism junctions, in an otherwise continuous structure. With maturation, the prism junction acquires a concentrated organic matrix acting as crack stoppers in the adult tooth.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Place orthodontic separators between the contact point of the primary molar for 4 or 5 days to create room for the crown. Select the smallest-sized crown covering all the cusps, approaching the contact points, and giving a 'spring back' effect. Adjustments of the crown are to be made using band-forming pliers if necessary. Cementation of the crown with glass ionomer cement. The tooth must be maintained in occlusion until the cement sets, followed by excess removal using floss. [25] [26] The Hall technique should be chosen as an alternative when the traditional approach with crown preparation is implausible. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjacent Stainless Steel Crown With Arch Length Loss/Space Loss
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction of the crown's mesiodistal dimension is frequently required, particularly when mesial drift (often caused by caries) has resulted in arch length loss. Flattening the crown's mesial and distal contact areas using Adam pliers results in a moderate reduction in the mesiodistal dimension. A contralateral upper tooth crown with a smaller size can be used if there is mesial drift in the lower arch. Another form of modification is vertical slicing and spot-welding, an additional segment of a stainless steel band. This is done to increase the perimeter or extend the length. However, the efficacy of the latter modifications remains largely untested. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Occlusal Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Operators can use 69L or 169L bur to reduce 1.5 to 2.0 mm of the occlusal surface. The cusps original contour needs to be preserved. Occlusal depth guidance grooves could be given for better reduction. If a sufficient amount of occlusal surface has already been lost due to caries, marginal ridges of neighboring teeth can be used as a reference. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of cerclage from cervix is a medical intervention that is directly related to the cervical cerclage procedure (إجراء تطويق عنق الرحم). This step involves extracting the sutures that were placed around the cervix to reinforce cervical competence and prevent premature dilation, and it represents a specific phase within the overall management of cervical insufficiency. Recognizing the connection between the removal of the cerclage and the broader cerclage procedure is essential for appropriate patient care and timing of intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2432
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1447
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) and REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR are both clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم. قصور عنق الرحم, also known as cervical insufficiency, is a condition in which the cervix is unable to maintain its structural integrity during pregnancy, increasing the risk of premature dilation and preterm delivery. The cerclage procedure involves placing a suturing stitch around the cervix to reinforce it and prevent premature opening, thereby directly addressing the mechanical weakness characteristic of قصور عنق الرحم. Additionally, a poor reproductive history—such as prior miscarriages, preterm births, or other adverse pregnancy outcomes—has been linked to a higher likelihood of developing قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that past obstetric complications can serve as a clinical indicator for this cervical disorder. Recognizing both the therapeutic role of cerclage and the predictive value of reproductive history is essential for early identification and management of patients at risk for قصور عنق الرحم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: إنهاء الحمل - اختياري, which denotes an elective termination of pregnancy, is clinically associated with تهديد بالإجهاض, a condition described as threatened abortion. This clinical association indicates that the two obstetric entities share overlapping concerns, and that the presence of a threatened abortion may influence considerations regarding elective pregnancy termination in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2820
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم, a condition in which the uterine cervix is unable to maintain sufficient closure during pregnancy, leading to an increased risk of preterm birth or pregnancy loss. The cerclage technique involves placing a suturing material around the cervix to reinforce its structural integrity, thereby preventing premature cervical dilation. This intervention is employed as a therapeutic measure to manage cervical insufficiency and improve obstetric outcomes for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1939
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior repair of vagina is a surgical procedure that directly involves the vagina, addressing defects or prolapse of the posterior vaginal wall to restore normal anatomy and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 918
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical canal structure includes the Structure of internal os as a parent anatomical component, indicating that the internal os is a key part of the cervical canal. Within obstetric care, the Repair of internal os of cervix is classified under other therapeutic obstetrical procedures and is performed directly on the Structure of internal os. Cervical cerclage of internal os during pregnancy is a specific type of Repair of internal os of cervix, representing a targeted intervention that reinforces the internal os to maintain cervical competence during pregnancy. Together, these concepts illustrate how the internal os serves both as an anatomical landmark within the cervical canal and as the direct site for therapeutic procedures aimed at preventing cervical insufficiency in pregnant patients.
